title: Two US Troops Killed in Jordan as Iran War Escalates, Canada Wildfires Trigger Tariff Threats, Hungary Ousts Orbán-Era President
subhead: Iranian strikes kill American service members for the first time since the MoU collapse, Canada's wildfire emergency draws Trump tariff threats and blankets US cities in smoke, and Hungary's post-Orbán government removes the last major figure of the old regime.

sections.heading: US Troops Killed in Iranian Strike on Jordan
sections.body:
Two US service members were killed and one remains missing after Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks struck US positions in Jordan on Friday, US Central Command confirmed. Four additional personnel were medically evacuated to Jordanian hospitals and have since been discharged. The deaths bring the total US military toll in the Iran conflict to 16, including a Navy pilot declared dead earlier this week.

The attack marks the first confirmed American combat fatalities from Iran's renewed offensive since the June memorandum of understanding collapsed. Iran's IRGC separately claimed it destroyed two US fighter aircraft at Jordan's Al-Azraq air base. Centcom declined to comment on that claim.

The US responded with an eighth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, which Centcom said targeted 'surveillance sites, military logistics infrastructure, underground weapons storage and maritime capabilities.' The State Department simultaneously issued a worldwide travel advisory urging 'increased caution' for Americans, particularly in the Middle East, citing 'the potential for unforeseen escalation.'
sections.whyItMatters: American combat deaths significantly raise the domestic political stakes for Trump, who faces midterm elections in November. Each escalation narrows the off-ramp for both sides and increases pressure for either a decisive military response or a diplomatic breakthrough.

sections.heading: Iran Declares MoU 'Suspended,' Khamenei Warns of 'Unforgettable Lessons'
sections.body:
Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi declared on Saturday that the memorandum of understanding with the United States is 'suspended,' citing repeated US violations, and said Tehran had likewise suspended all its own commitments under the deal. The statement effectively confirms what both sides have been acting on for over a week.

Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who has not appeared in public since the attack that killed his father at the start of the war, issued a written statement read on state television calling Trump's signature 'utterly worthless and invalid' and warning that Iran and its allies hold 'unforgettable lessons' for the US. He accused Washington of seeking to 'escalate the conflict' and called on Iranians to remain 'vigilant and active.'

Iranian strikes hit civilian infrastructure across the Gulf this week: Kuwait confirmed a power plant and water desalination facility were struck, and Jordan said it intercepted 10 Iranian missiles in its airspace. The Gulf Cooperation Council's secretary-general called Iran's targeting of civilian infrastructure a 'war crime.' Satellite images also emerged showing damage inside Iran's Bushehr nuclear power plant, though the extent and cause remain unclear.
sections.whyItMatters: The formal suspension of the MoU eliminates even the rhetorical framework for de-escalation. With Iran striking Gulf state infrastructure and the US hitting Iranian bridges and water plants, the conflict has shifted from military-on-military exchanges to mutual targeting of civilian and economic infrastructure — a pattern that deepens civilian hardship and makes negotiation harder.

sections.heading: Canada's Wildfire Crisis Crosses the Border
sections.body:
Nearly 1,000 wildfires are burning across Canada — 955 as of Saturday, according to the Canadian Interagency Forest Fire Centre, with roughly 200 of those in Ontario alone. Smoke has blanketed cities across eastern North America, with Toronto, New York, and Washington DC recording among the worst air quality levels in the world on Saturday. A First Nations community in northern Ontario was 'completely levelled' by fire, according to an incident commander.

President Trump threatened to impose new tariffs on Canada over what he called the country's 'willful negligence' in forest management, writing on Truth Social that 'the United States is being unnecessarily invaded by filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air.' Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney responded that climate change is 'everyone's responsibility,' while Ontario Premier Doug Ford called Trump's rhetoric 'a shame' and 'unacceptable,' noting that Canada sent firefighters to help battle US wildfires in California last year.

The haze has disrupted daily life across the northeastern US: outdoor events cancelled, masks distributed in cities, flights delayed. Forecasters expect rain and shifting winds to improve air quality by Sunday, easing concerns around the World Cup final at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey.
sections.whyItMatters: A climate-driven disaster is now a diplomatic flashpoint. Trump's tariff threat turns an environmental crisis into a trade confrontation, and the scale of the fires — nearly 3 million hectares burned already — underscores how wildfire seasons are intensifying faster than any single country's capacity to manage them.

sections.heading: Ukraine Drones Hit Russia's Biggest Online Retailer
sections.body:
Ukrainian drone strikes killed eight people and injured 62 at two warehouses belonging to Wildberries — Russia's largest online retailer, often described as the Russian equivalent of Amazon — in the Moscow region and Tambov, roughly 475km southeast of Moscow. Seven people died in Tambov and one in Elektrostal, with dozens injured by shrapnel.

President Zelensky said the targets were 'major logistics facilities' used to 'supply sanctioned components for drone production and navigation equipment.' A Ukrainian security analyst told the BBC that Wildberries' platform had been used by Russian volunteers to purchase military equipment, body armour, and drone parts. An oil depot in the Moscow region was also struck by a falling drone.

The strikes coincide with a deepening economic strain inside Russia. The BBC reports that Russians added 1.56 trillion roubles ($20bn) in cash into circulation since January — the largest increase outside the pandemic — driven by mobile internet shutdowns that disrupt card payments and businesses seeking to dodge the VAT hike from 20% to 22%. Sberbank's CFO warned that cash is not returning to the banking system, staying 'in people's hands,' a sign of a growing shadow economy.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is broadening its drone campaign beyond oil infrastructure to Russia's commercial logistics backbone. Combined with the accelerating cash economy and tax evasion, the strikes aim at both military supply chains and the economic foundations sustaining the war.

sections.heading: Hungary Removes Orbán-Era President
sections.body:
Hungary's President Tamás Sulyok agreed to step down on Saturday, signing a constitutional amendment that ends his presidency at midnight Sunday. Sulyok, widely seen as a loyalist of former Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, had faced a five-day deadline to sign or trigger a constitutional crisis and impeachment.

The amendment was steamrolled through parliament by Prime Minister Péter Magyar's Tisza party following its landslide April victory over Orbán's Fidesz. Sulyok condemned the move as a 'breaking point in Hungarian constitutional democracy' and accused the new government of trampling core democratic values — but signed anyway.

Orbán, who governed Hungary for 16 years before his shock defeat, has rarely been seen in public since April and has refused to take his parliamentary seat. A former Supreme Court head told the BBC that while he agreed with Sulyok's removal, dismantling Orbán's 'sophisticated authoritarian regime' remains difficult because it 'was designed to survive even after electoral defeat.'
sections.whyItMatters: Sulyok's removal clears the last major Orbán-appointed figure from a position of authority, but the manner of his ouster — a forced constitutional amendment — raises questions about whether Hungary's democratic restoration is building new institutions or simply replacing one dominant party's grip with another.

sections.heading: Germany's Spahn Resigns Over Surrogacy Controversy
sections.body:
Jens Spahn, one of the most powerful figures in Germany's governing CDU and parliamentary group leader, resigned on Saturday after revelations that he and his husband had a child through a surrogate mother in the United States. Surrogacy is banned in Germany — a prohibition that Spahn's own party reaffirmed in a February resolution, and that Spahn personally supported as health minister in 2020.

Chancellor Friedrich Merz called the resignation 'right' and 'inevitable,' citing credibility as 'the highest asset in politics.' Spahn wrote that 'my personal happiness is not compatible with my political office,' acknowledging the contradiction but also criticising the 'increasing relentlessness in public discourse.'

The resignation comes at a delicate moment for Merz, who is struggling in polls ahead of key regional elections in Saxony-Anhalt this autumn, where the far-right AfD could win an outright majority — which would be the first time a far-right party has held power in a German state since World War Two.
sections.whyItMatters: Spahn's fall removes the most prominent internal challenger to Merz within the CDU, but the hypocrisy angle — a senior figure using an arrangement he helped deny to others — feeds the populist narrative that establishment politicians operate by different rules, directly ahead of elections where that narrative could hand the AfD a historic win.

bottomLine: The Iran-US conflict crossed a significant threshold with the first confirmed American combat deaths since the MoU collapsed, while both sides have abandoned even the pretense of a diplomatic framework — Iran formally suspended the deal, and the US entered its eighth straight night of strikes. The targeting of civilian infrastructure is now mutual and expanding, from Iranian strikes on Kuwaiti desalination plants to US attacks on Iranian bridges and water facilities. Separately, Canada's wildfire emergency has become a cross-border political crisis, with Trump threatening tariffs over smoke-choked US cities. In Europe, Hungary completed its post-Orbán transition by removing the last major Fidesz appointee, and Germany's governing coalition lost its most ambitious internal figure to a surrogacy scandal weeks before critical state elections.
